title: Managing Context Window Effectively
content:
Claude Code has a large but finite context window. To use it effectively:

- **Be selective with file reads**: Use `offset` and `limit` on Read for large files. Don't read entire files when you only need a section.
- **Use Grep before Read**: Find the right files with Grep, then Read only the relevant ones.
- **Compact regularly**: Use `/compact` when the conversation gets long to summarize and free up context.
- **Start new conversations**: For unrelated tasks, start a fresh conversation rather than continuing a long one.
- **Reference files with @**: Use `@filename` to include file contents efficiently — Claude reads them automatically.

The context window includes everything: system prompts, CLAUDE.md, conversation history, tool results, and your messages. Be mindful of what's consuming space.

title: CLAUDE.md Best Practices
content:
CLAUDE.md is your project's instruction file — Claude reads it at the start of every conversation. Make it count:

- **Project overview**: Brief description of what the project is and its tech stack.
- **Key conventions**: Naming conventions, file organization patterns, preferred libraries.
- **Build/test commands**: How to build, test, lint, and run the project.
- **Important rules**: Things Claude should always or never do in this project.
- **Architecture notes**: Key architectural decisions and why they were made.

Keep it concise — everything in CLAUDE.md consumes context window. Put detailed docs in separate files and reference them.

You can have CLAUDE.md files at multiple levels:
- `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` — global, applies to all projects
- `/project/CLAUDE.md` — project root, applies to the project
- `/project/src/CLAUDE.md` — directory-specific, applies when working in that directory

title: Using Claude's Memory System
content:
Claude Code has a memory system that persists information across conversations:

- **Auto-memory**: Claude stores useful context in `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/` as markdown files.
- **Memory index**: A `MEMORY.md` file indexes all memory files with descriptions.
- **Project memory**: Specific to each project directory — different projects have separate memories.
- **User memory**: Global preferences stored at the user level.

To leverage memory effectively:
- Let Claude save important decisions and preferences automatically.
- You can manually edit memory files to correct or add information.
- Reference memory in your prompts: "Remember that we decided to use..." — Claude will check its memory.
- Clear outdated memory when project direction changes significantly.

title: MCP Servers: Extending Claude's Capabilities
content:
Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers extend Claude Code with custom tools and data sources:

- **Built-in MCP tools**: GitHub operations, database queries, and more are available via MCP.
- **Configuration**: MCP servers are configured in `.claude/settings.json` at project or user level.
- **Deferred tools**: MCP tools appear as deferred — use `ToolSearch` to fetch their schemas before calling them.
- **Custom servers**: You can build custom MCP servers to give Claude access to your specific tools and data.

Common MCP integrations:
- **GitHub**: Create PRs, issues, review code directly through Claude.
- **PostgreSQL**: Query databases directly for debugging or analysis.
- **Custom APIs**: Build MCP servers that wrap your internal APIs.

MCP tools show up in system-reminder messages when available in a session.

title: Hooks: Automating Before and After Actions
content:
Claude Code supports hooks that run automatically before or after certain actions:

- **Pre-commit hooks**: Run linting, formatting, or validation before commits.
- **Post-tool hooks**: Run actions after specific tools execute.
- **Custom hooks**: Define your own hooks for project-specific workflows.

Configure hooks via `/update-config` or directly in settings files. Common uses:
- Auto-format code after every Edit or Write operation.
- Run type-checking before commits.
- Auto-restart dev server after config changes.
- Validate test coverage before allowing commits.

Hooks should be fast — slow hooks will make the workflow feel sluggish.

title: Permission Modes: Balancing Speed and Safety
content:
Claude Code has permission modes that control what actions require user approval:

- **Ask mode** (default): Claude asks for permission before file writes, command execution, and other side effects.
- **Auto-accept mode**: Automatically approves safe operations. Configure which operations are auto-approved.
- **Plan mode**: Read-only — Claude can explore and plan but not make changes. Toggle with Shift+Tab.

For experienced users, auto-accept mode with a well-configured allowlist dramatically speeds up workflows. You can auto-approve:
- File edits in specific directories
- Specific shell commands (npm test, git status, etc.)
- Read operations (always safe)

Keep destructive operations (git push, rm, database writes) on manual approval.

title: Delegating Work to Sub-Agents
content:
The Agent tool lets you spawn sub-agents for parallel or isolated work:

**When to delegate:**
- Exploring multiple code paths simultaneously
- Running experiments in isolation (worktree agents)
- Long-running research while you continue working
- Tasks that are independent and can run in parallel

**Best practices:**
- Give agents focused, specific tasks — not vague directives.
- Use 2-3 parallel agents rather than 1 overloaded agent.
- Background agents for long tasks, foreground for quick ones.
- Worktree agents for risky experiments that might break things.

**Example workflow:**
1. Spawn an explore agent to understand the auth module.
2. Spawn another to understand the database schema.
3. Once both complete, use their findings to plan your implementation.

title: Using @ File References Effectively
content:
The `@` syntax is one of Claude Code's most powerful features:

- **@filename**: Reference a file to include its contents in context automatically.
- **Autocomplete**: After typing @, you get a dropdown of matching files.
- **Multiple files**: Reference several files in one prompt: `@src/auth.ts @src/middleware.ts`
- **Directories**: Some implementations support `@src/` to reference a directory.

**When to use @:**
- When you want Claude to see the current state of a file without a separate Read call.
- When asking Claude to modify a file — @reference ensures it has the latest content.
- When comparing files — reference both in your prompt.

**Tip**: Use @ references instead of pasting code — it's more efficient and ensures Claude sees the actual file, not an outdated copy.

title: Writing Effective Prompts for Claude Code
content:
The way you prompt Claude Code significantly affects output quality:

**Be specific:**
- Bad: "Fix the bug"
- Good: "Fix the login redirect loop in src/auth/callback.ts — users get stuck after OAuth callback"

**Provide context:**
- Mention relevant files, components, or modules.
- Describe the expected behavior vs actual behavior.
- Include error messages or stack traces.

**Set constraints:**
- "Use the existing pattern in src/api/users.ts as a reference"
- "Don't modify the database schema"
- "Keep the changes backward compatible"

**Ask for what you want:**
- "Explain your reasoning before making changes"
- "Show me the plan before implementing"
- "Make the minimal change necessary"

**One thing at a time:**
- Break complex tasks into steps rather than asking for everything at once.
- Each focused request gets better results than one massive request.

title: When to Use Plan Mode
content:
Plan mode (Shift+Tab) restricts Claude to read-only operations — perfect for:

**Use Plan mode when:**
- Starting a complex task — understand before you act.
- Investigating a bug — explore without accidentally changing things.
- Reviewing architecture — analyze without modifying.
- Learning a codebase — safe exploration.
- You want a plan before implementation.

**Plan mode workflow:**
1. Enter Plan mode (Shift+Tab)
2. Ask Claude to explore, analyze, and create a plan
3. Review the plan and ask clarifying questions
4. Exit Plan mode (Shift+Tab again)
5. Say "Execute the plan" to implement

**Tip**: Plan mode is especially valuable for large refactors where making changes without understanding the full scope could create problems.

title: Git Worktree Isolation for Safe Experiments
content:
Worktrees give you isolated working directories linked to the same repo:

**What worktrees do:**
- Create a separate directory with its own working tree.
- Share the same git history and branches.
- Changes in the worktree don't affect your main directory.
- Can be committed, merged, or discarded.

**When to use worktrees:**
- Trying a risky refactoring approach.
- A/B testing two different implementations.
- Running a potentially breaking experiment.
- Working on a feature while keeping main directory clean.

**How to use:**
- Use `EnterWorktree` tool or Agent with `worktree: true`.
- Make changes and test in isolation.
- If changes work, commit and merge. If not, discard.
- Always `ExitWorktree` to clean up.

Worktrees are the safety net that makes bold experiments free.

title: Running Background Tasks Effectively
content:
Background tasks let you do multiple things simultaneously:

**Starting background tasks:**
- `Bash` with `run_in_background: true` — for shell commands.
- `TaskCreate` — for tracked background tasks.
- `Agent` with `run_in_background: true` — for background agents.

**Common background uses:**
- Running test suites while continuing development.
- Starting dev servers.
- Building the project while working on other files.
- Running lint/format across the entire codebase.

**Monitoring:**
- You get notified when background tasks complete.
- Use `TaskGet` to check status, `TaskOutput` to read results.
- Use `TaskList` to see all active tasks.

**Tip**: Don't poll for task completion with sleep loops — you'll be notified automatically. Just continue working and check results when notified.

title: Mastering Slash Commands
content:
Slash commands (skills) are pre-built workflows for common tasks:

**Essential commands:**
- `/commit` — create well-formatted git commits.
- `/review-pr` — thorough pull request reviews.
- `/simplify` — code quality review and simplification.
- `/loop` — run a command repeatedly, fixing issues until it passes.

**Built-in commands:**
- `/help` — show available commands and help.
- `/clear` — clear the conversation.
- `/compact` — summarize and compress the conversation.
- `/status` — show current session status.

**Tips:**
- Type `/` to see all available commands.
- Commands can accept arguments: `/review-pr 42`.
- Installed skills (like GSD) add their own slash commands.
- You can also trigger skills with natural language — "commit my changes" invokes /commit.

title: Customizing Keybindings
content:
Claude Code's keyboard shortcuts can be customized:

**Default shortcuts to know:**
- `Shift+Enter` — newline in input.
- `Shift+Tab` — toggle plan/act mode.
- `Escape Escape` — undo last code change.
- `Ctrl+C` — cancel generation.
- `Ctrl+L` — clear conversation.
- `Up Arrow` — cycle input history.

**Customization:**
- Use `/keybindings-help` to see all shortcuts.
- Custom keybindings can be configured for frequently used actions.
- Keybindings can vary by context (input, conversation, generation).

**Tip**: Learning the shortcuts for mode switching (Shift+Tab) and history navigation (Up Arrow) will significantly speed up your workflow.

title: Setting Up a Project for Claude Code
content:
Proper project setup makes Claude Code dramatically more effective:

**Essential setup:**
1. Create `CLAUDE.md` in the project root with tech stack, conventions, and key commands.
2. Ensure `package.json` (or equivalent) has accurate scripts for build, test, lint, dev.
3. Have a `.gitignore` that excludes build artifacts, node_modules, and secrets.

**Recommended setup:**
- Configure MCP servers for your tools (GitHub, databases, etc.).
- Set up permission auto-approvals for common safe operations.
- Create directory-level CLAUDE.md files for complex sub-projects.
- Configure hooks for auto-formatting or auto-testing.

**What to include in CLAUDE.md:**
```
# Project: MyApp
Tech: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL

## Commands
- `npm run dev` — start dev server
- `npm test` — run tests
- `npm run lint` — run linter

## Conventions
- Use server components by default
- API routes in /app/api/
- Zod for validation
```

title: Git Workflows with Claude Code
content:
Claude Code excels at git operations — here's how to use it effectively:

**Committing:**
- Use `/commit` for well-formatted commits that match your project's style.
- Claude analyzes the diff and writes meaningful commit messages.
- Sensitive files (.env, credentials) are flagged before committing.

**Pull Requests:**
- Use `/review-pr` for thorough code reviews.
- Ask Claude to create PRs with `gh pr create` — it generates good descriptions.
- Claude checks ALL commits in a PR, not just the latest.

**Branching:**
- Ask Claude to create feature branches before starting work.
- Use worktrees for experimental branches.

**Safety:**
- Claude never force-pushes to main/master.
- Destructive git operations require explicit user approval.
- Pre-commit hooks are always respected.

**Tip**: Let Claude handle git operations — it's careful about safety and produces clean history.

title: Test-Driven Development with Claude
content:
Claude Code is excellent for TDD workflows:

**TDD flow:**
1. Write a failing test (or ask Claude to write it from requirements).
2. Use `/loop npm test` to run tests in a loop.
3. Claude implements code to make the test pass.
4. Claude automatically refactors while keeping tests green.

**Effective TDD prompting:**
- "Write a failing test for [feature], then implement it."
- "Here's my test — make it pass with the simplest implementation."
- "The tests pass, now refactor the implementation without breaking them."

**Tips:**
- Start with the simplest test case, then add complexity.
- Let Claude generate edge case tests after the happy path works.
- Use `/loop` to automate the red-green-refactor cycle.
- Tests serve as specification — be precise about expected behavior.

title: Strategies for Large Codebases
content:
Large codebases need different strategies than small projects:

**Navigation:**
- Use Grep with type/glob filters to narrow searches.
- Use Glob to find files by pattern rather than browsing directories.
- Read specific sections of large files with offset/limit.
- Use @ references for files you're working with.

**Context management:**
- Don't try to read the entire codebase — be surgical.
- Use `/compact` frequently to free up context.
- Start new conversations for unrelated tasks.
- Use CLAUDE.md to give Claude architectural context without reading everything.

**Exploration:**
- Use Agent with type 'explore' for codebase mapping.
- Start broad (directory structure) then narrow to specific areas.
- Use LSP for precise code navigation (go-to-definition, find-references).

**Editing:**
- Make changes in small, focused batches.
- Test after each batch of changes.
- Use worktrees for large refactors that touch many files.

title: Making Multi-File Edits Safely
content:
When a change spans multiple files, careful coordination is essential:

**Strategy:**
1. Plan the change in Plan mode first — identify ALL files that need to change.
2. Make changes in dependency order — types/interfaces first, then implementations, then consumers.
3. Run tests after each logically complete set of changes.

**Best practices:**
- Use Grep to find all places that reference what you're changing.
- Update imports and type definitions before updating implementation.
- Check for string references (error messages, logs) that might need updating.
- Run the full test suite after completing all changes.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Forgetting to update tests when changing function signatures.
- Missing string-based references (dynamic imports, config references).
- Not updating re-exports in index files.

**Tip**: Ask Claude to "find all files that need to change for [your modification]" before making any edits — this prevents partial migrations.

title: Claude Code with Different Providers
content:
Claude Code can work with different AI providers and model configurations:

- **Default**: Uses Anthropic's Claude API directly.
- **AWS Bedrock**: Can be configured to use Claude via AWS Bedrock.
- **Google Vertex AI**: Supports Claude via Google Vertex.
- **API keys**: Configure via environment variables or settings.

**Model selection:**
- Different tasks may benefit from different models.
- Larger context models handle bigger codebases better.
- Faster models are better for quick iterations.

**Cost management:**
- Be mindful of context window usage — large contexts cost more.
- Use `/compact` to reduce ongoing costs in long conversations.
- Background agents consume tokens independently.

**Tip**: For cost-sensitive workflows, use smaller context operations (targeted Grep + Read) rather than loading entire files into context.
